frameworks.name: prediction-for-perception
frameworks.one_line: Brain predicts incoming stimuli to facilitate categorization

frameworks.name: feature-specific predictive coding
frameworks.one_line: Predictions encode specific category features rather than category contrasts

frameworks.name: category-contrast prediction
frameworks.one_line: Typical underconstrained two-category contrast formulation of predictions

frameworks.name: MEG multivariate decoding
frameworks.one_line: Classifier decodes feature-tied perceptions from dynamic MEG responses

core_question: What are the information contents of perceptual predictions, and how do they bias subsequent decisions?
key_assumptions: Same stimuli can support two distinct categorical perceptions tied to different features
Multivariate classifiers can read out feature-specific neural representations from MEG
Auditory cues can elicit pre-stimulus visual category predictions
manipulations: Auditory cueing of visual category before stimulus
Compared feature-specific vs typical category-contrast predictions
Examined pre-stimulus reactivation timing and localization
key_findings: Brain reactivates visual category features before stimulus onset following auditory cues
Feature-specific predictions are more lateralized than category-contrast predictions
Feature predictions are more specifically driven by the auditory cues
Pre-stimulus reactivation strength of feature predictions biases later categorization
interpretations: Predictions carry specific feature content rather than just category contrasts
Strength of predicted feature information directly biases perceptual decisions
